### Post by revi_2003 on 2007-11-02
I have a 22-inch Acer AL2223W monitor and have been trying to set the resolution to display at 1680x1050(the native resolution). However, I've had no success. The following is my xorf.conf file:
# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated

Section "Files"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"

	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc104"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"

	BusID		"PCI:0:2:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"

	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"Intel Corporation 82845G/GL[Brookdale-G]/GE Chipset Integrated Graphics Device"
	Monitor		"AL2223W"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1680x1050" "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1680x1050" "1280x1024" "1024x768" "832x624" "800x600" "720x400" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8

	EndSubSection

	EndSubSection

	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"

	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666

### Post by revi_2003 on 2007-11-02
just realized that i need to set the frequency to 60hz instead of the 75hz that it is on now.How to I do that?

I am running Ubuntu 5.10.

Please someone help!

### Post by Arthur Archnix on 2007-11-02
Sorry, did you write 5.10 or... yeah. Did you mean to do that?

---

### Post by revi_2003 on 2007-11-02
yeah. I did mean to do that. something wrong? Im new to linux/Ubuntu.

---

### Post by mikewhatever on 2007-11-02
As you can see, the resolution you want is not in xorg.conf. Add it manually, save and exit. Then restart x (Alt+Ctrl+Del).

---

### Post by alienexplorers on 2007-11-02
Try adding the following line to your monitor section of your xorg.conf file:
> Modeline "1680x1050_60.00"  147.14  1680 1784 1968 2256  1050 1051 1054 1087  -HSync +Vsync

---

### Post by Arthur Archnix on 2007-11-02
Sounds like 6.06 is the version you want then. It's newish, well supported, perfect for your hardware, and very stable which is appropriate for a machine you want to use as a server.

You can download it [here]("http://www.ubuntu.com/getubuntu/download"). Just remember to choose the 6.06 LTS version.

### Post by Arthur Archnix on 2007-11-02
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg
```
then ctrl+alt+backspsace to restart x. Should get you back to where you were before.

### Post by Inxsible on 2007-11-02

### Post by Arthur Archnix on 2007-11-02
I recommended dapper because he was using 5.10 and only complaining about resolution, so chances are he's on an older 2 or 3 year old machine and the latest hardware isn't really an issue. He's only got 512MB of RAM, so you probably don't want to run a composited OS by default. Lastly, he wants to use the system as a server in the future, so stability is important.

It's a toss-up between feisty and dapper in my humble opinion.

---

### Post by revi_2003 on 2007-11-02

What the reason behind long-time support? Besides wouldn't everyone want to be behind a product that has long support?

He's only got 512MB of RAM, so you probably don't want to run a composited OS by default. Lastly, he wants to use the system as a server in the future, so stability is important.

It's a toss-up between feisty and dapper in my humble opinion.

That's why I suggested using Xubuntu because he has less RAM. although it will run Ubuntu easily, he might get better performance with Xubuntu.

### Post by akiratheoni on 2007-11-02
> **revi_2003 said:**
> So.....what are the differences between 6.06 and 7.10?

What the reason behind long-time support? Besides wouldn't everyone want to be behind a product that has long support?

Well, 6.06 doesn't have the cutting edge features that 7.10 does... and if I remember correctly, 6.06's support will run out at the same time that 7.10 does, so if your computer can run 7.10, it's best to get that.

If you get 7.10, though, I will tell you that my 1680x1050 resolution was detected by either using 915resolution (but that's obsolete, and I only used that with 7.04), or with the newer intel driver.

Also, it was sometimes detected when I used the dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg and UNSELECTED 1680x1050 from the available resolutions... no idea why, but it worked.

---

### Post by Inxsible on 2007-11-02
> **revi_2003 said:**
> So.....what are the differences between 6.06 and 7.10?

What the reason behind long-time support? Besides wouldn't everyone want to be behind a product that has long support?
All new versions have an 18 month support. The ones released with the LTS tag or Long Term Support have a 3 year support for desktop and 5 year for server editions.

Now, its upto you to decide what kind of support you want. Remember that a new version is released every 6 months.

I, for one, like to upgrade as soon as I am sure the new one is better at handling a few things.

for eg. In 7.04, suspend and hibernate did not work for me. also my cpu fan was constantly running. I installed 7.10 in a different partition and found that it could suspend, hibernate and over the course of time I also noticed that it ran much quieter than 7.04. That in itself was sufficient for me to change over.

Once I got 7.10, customized to the way I like it, I got rid of 7.04 :)

---

### Post by eye208 on 2007-11-02
> **revi_2003 said:**
> What the reason behind long-time support? Besides wouldn't everyone want to be behind a product that has long support?
"Long-term support" is all about marketing. It makes business users feel good because it means they need not touch their servers for five years.

But in the real world, sticking with an old version of Linux does not really make sense. Ubuntu 6.06 comes with outdated applications and outdated drivers. If you installed it back in 2006 and still use it because it works for you, that's fine. But I would not recommend to do a fresh install of Ubuntu 6.06 today.

---

### Post by observative on 2008-01-05
Hi, I was reading this thread because I have the same monitor, different vid card, but the same issue. And since you brought up the os version, I thought I would add that I am running Ubuntu Gusty Gibbon on my PIII 1.0ghz with 384mb of ram, and it runs smooth even on videos like HD mkv's.

The main reason I'm posting is because I got my monitor to work, the Acer AL2223W, and I did it with some help from here, [http://forum.tuxx-home.at/viewtopic.php?f=1&t=327&p=3140](http://forum.tuxx-home.at/viewtopic.php?f=1&t=327&p=3140). Until I got the xorg.conf file correct, and all the info needed for the vid card and monitor, I could get the res to display and then it would change when I rebooted. But after making some mods to the xorg as suggested in the link above it worked like a champ.

So may I suggest going there, download the attached xorg.conf files and see if you can "merge" with what's in your xorg.conf. I think you will get it working if you do. I tried literally hundreds of suggestions over the past week and this was the only one that truly worked...the first time I tried it, and it still is after several reboots, updates, and software installs.

Good luck!

---
